retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Insulin Metabolic Effects -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin is an anabolic hormone that elicits metabolic effects throughout the body. In the pancreas, endocrine tissue known as the islets of Langerhans contains beta cells. Beta cells are responsible for insulin synthesis. Beta cells regulate insulin production by monitoring glucose levels, amino acids, keto acids, and fatty acids circulating within the plasma. Insulin’s overall role is controlling energy conservation and utilization during feeding and fasting. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the duration of action, insulin is categorized as short-acting, intermediate-acting, and long-acting.

retrieved_docs.title: Aspart Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart regulates glucose metabolism by promoting storage and inhibiting the breakdown of glucose, fat, and amino acids. Insulin reduces blood glucose levels by increasing peripheral glucose uptake, particularly in skeletal muscle and fat. Insulin enhances the storage of fat (lipogenesis) and protein synthesis. Insulin aspart also inhibits gluconeogenesis (hepatic glucose production), lipolysis (breakdown of lipids to fatty acids), and proteolysis (breakdown of proteins into amino acids). Maximum glucose-reducing effects occur within 1 to 3 hours and last 3 to 5 hours. Insulin aspart is equipotent to regular insulin but has a faster onset and shorter duration of action. Thus, insulin aspart is preferred for mealtime insulin coverage as it can be administered every 4 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin Lispro -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin lispro is a rapid-acting, human insulin analog that works parenterally to lower blood glucose by regulating the metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. Insulin lispro works by binding to a glycoprotein receptor specific to insulin on the surface of target cells. These receptors are found on various target tissues, including adipose tissue, skeletal muscle, and the liver. [7] In adipose tissue, insulin produces free fatty acids by supporting the processing of circulating lipoproteins. This also helps with the synthesis of triglycerides, as well as adipocyte storage. Insulin also directly inhibits the hydrolysis of triglycerides. In the liver, insulin stimulates hepatic glycogen synthesis and promotes the synthesis of fatty acids. In skeletal muscle, insulin increases the synthesis of protein and glycogen.

retrieved_docs.title: Regular Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Decreasing proteolysis is the major effect of insulin on the human body in terms of protein metabolism. These effects are dose-dependent. Essential amino acids are more sensitive to insulin´s effect; therefore, in the presence of euglycemia and hypoaminoacidemia, the effects of insulin are effective in decreasing proteolysis. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Aspart Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The pancreas naturally produces endogenous insulin. Insulin apart is manufactured through recombinant DNA techniques using the Saccharomyces cerevisiae expression system . Insulin aspart differs from endogenous insulin as it has an aspartic acid residue at position B28 instead of proline. Insulins can be categorized as rapid, short, intermediate, and long-acting depending on their pharmacologic effects (eg, onset, peak, and duration).  Rapid-acting insulin aspart has a quicker onset of action than insulin aspart or short-acting (regular) insulin. The formulation of insulin aspart includes 2 excipients: niacinamide and L-arginine. Niacinamide has a faster initial absorption following subcutaneous administration, while L-arginine is a stabilizing agent. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: NPH Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The intermediate-acting NPH insulin is obtained from the precipitation of recombinant synthesized human insulin with zinc in the presence of protamine, a basic polyarginine peptide. This process occurs at a neutral pH in an insulin and protamine ratio of 5: 1. This formulation method gives NPH the property of protraction, i.e., slow release of insulin from the precipitated insulin present at the injection site after subcutaneous injection. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5] After injecting the protein-insulin complex, the solvent from NPH insulin suspension diffuses into the subcutaneous tissue. These insulin crystals are stacked up like a heap at the injection depot and dissolve gradually, leading to the protracted action of NPH.

retrieved_docs.title: Glargine Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin glargine has an onset of action of 1.5 to 2 hours. It has a long duration of action of up to 24 hours due to modifications of amino acids, including Asn to Gly at position 21 of the A chain and the addition of 2 Arg residues and positions 31 and 32 of the B chain. This arrangement allows the insulin to remain soluble at a pH of 4.0, the pH of the solution in which it is administered, and then become insoluble at physiologic pH. Once insulin glargine is injected into the body and introduced to the higher pH, the insoluble precipitate that forms slowly releases soluble protein over 24 hours. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Regular Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In states of prolonged fasting or uncontrolled diabetes, such as DKA, in which insulin levels are low, FFA is released into circulation by lipolysis in addition to an increased hepatic FFA oxidation to ketone bodies (B-hydroxybutyrate and acetate) which results in ketonemia and metabolic acidosis. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Regular Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin, a natural endogenous hormone synthesized and secreted by pancreatic beta cells, has a structure of 51 amino acids.

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid-acting insulins (lispro and aspart) start their action in 5 to 15 minutes and peak in 30 minutes. The duration of action is 3 to 5 hours. They are generally used before meals and are always used along with short-acting or long-acting insulins to control sugar levels throughout the day.

retrieved_docs.title: Human Insulin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with diabetes mellitus, to reach the goal of normal 24-hour insulin activity like in healthy adults without diabetes mellitus, one single insulin formulation with a defined onset, peak, and duration of action is not helpful. Hence there is a need for different kinds of insulin that have different pharmacokinetics. Based on the mode of action, there are four different types of insulin analogs as follows [8] :

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Insulin Metabolic Effects -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin is a peptide hormone comprised of 51 amino acids distributed among 2 peptide chains, the A and B chains, of 21 and 30 amino acid residues, respectively. Disulfide bonds of cysteine residues connect the 2 chains. Preproinsulin is the original precursor protein of insulin. It is a single-chain polypeptide consisting of proinsulin and signal peptide sequences. After translating into the endoplasmic reticulum, preproinsulin cleaves at its signal peptide, releasing proinsulin. Proinsulin is a single chain containing insulin’s A and B chains continuously, joined through a segment known as the C domain. Dibasic residues flank the C domain at each end. At the site of each dibasic residue, a trypsin-like enzyme cleaves proinsulin. This cleavage finally releases insulin along with a C-peptide. Until metabolically needed, insulin is stored within glucose-regulated secretory vesicles as zinc insulin hexamers. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin Pump -- Definition/Introduction -- Insulin Delivery
retrieved_docs.content: Mealtime Bolus: Insulin is also delivered at the time of meals as a mealtime bolus. Bolus is calculated based on the number of carbs entered by the patient. The insulin to carb ratio (ICR) has to be entered into the pump beforehand by the patient or their clinician and is calculated by the formula: ICR: 450/TDD. ICR reflects the number of grams of carbs covered by one unit of insulin. With insulin pumps, there is the option for different bolus profiles, including dual wave, short extended, and long extended boluses, which will be discussed below. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin Resistance -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The gold standard for measuring insulin resistance is the hyperinsulinemic-euglycemic glucose clamp technique. This is a research technique in which a fasting, non-diabetic patient is placed on a high-rate constant infusion of insulin to suppress hepatic glucose production; the blood glucose is frequently monitored while a concomitant 20% dextrose solution is given at varying rates to regulate the blood glucose in the euglycemic range. The amount of glucose required to reach a steady state reflects the exogenous glucose disposal needed to compensate for hyperinsulinemia. Insulin resistance calculation is based on whole-body glucose disposal and body size. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin Lispro -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: The bioavailability of insulin lispro after subcutaneous injection ranges from 55% to 77%. Insulin lispro-aabc demonstrates a slightly faster onset of action (15 to 18 minutes) than insulin lispro (23 to 27 minutes). However, both types of insulin have a comparable peak time of approximately 1 to 2 hours. Insulin lispro has a longer duration of action, lasting for around 5 hours, while insulin lispro-aabc exhibits a slightly shorter duration of action of approximately 4 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal categories: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Within the insulin subclass, formulation groups are defined. “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” comprises rapid‑acting analogue insulin aspart (human), which binds the human insulin receptor (INSR) and produces the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting an anabolic action on adipose tissue. “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” includes insulin glargine and the fixed‑dose combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide; both combinations integrate a basal insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue to enhance glycaemic control. “Insulin, Beef” is a protein entity classified within the same combined intermediate‑/long‑acting‑with‑fast‑acting group, illustrating the diversity of insulin sources.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 37
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human insulin analogue, is classified within the group “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting.” This class also includes beef insulin, indicating that both insulin aspart and insulin, beef are members of the same therapeutic category of injectable diabetes treatments. Insulin aspart exerts its action by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein) and produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting its role in suppressing the breakdown of fat. Together, these relationships highlight how insulin aspart functions at the molecular level and its placement among other injectable insulin preparations used in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2394
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human insulin analogue, is classified as a member of the group “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting,” indicating that it is employed in combination regimens with longer‑acting insulins for diabetes management. Mechanistically, insulin aspart exerts its glucose‑lowering effect by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein), the primary receptor mediating insulin’s metabolic actions. This dual characterization highlights both its therapeutic role within mixed‑insulin therapy and its specific molecular interaction with the INSR protein, informing its use in achieving optimal glycemic control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 267
retrieved_docs.content: insulin degludec/liraglutide contains the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutide, and insulin glargine/lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide. This integration of an insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue provides both basal insulin coverage and incretin‑mediated glucose control in a single injection. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies how diabetes pharmacotherapy is organized: insulin‑based therapies are distinguished from non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, and within each group, drugs are further classified by mechanism, duration of action, and formulation, facilitating precise selection and combination of therapies for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2185
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses insulins and analogues. Insulins and analogues represent a specific subclass of drugs within this category, providing essential hormone replacement and glucose‑lowering effects for patients with diabetes. Within the insulins and analogues subclass, the formulation described as Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate- or long-acting combined with fast-acting is a further specialized group. This group consists of injectable insulin preparations that combine intermediate‑ or long‑acting basal insulin with rapid‑acting insulin to achieve both steady background glucose control and prompt post‑prandial reduction. Understanding this hierarchical relationship clarifies how the specific injection regimen fits into the larger class of insulin therapies and the overall spectrum of drugs used in diabetes, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment strategies for optimal glycemic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 0 -- Community 17
retrieved_docs.content: Insulins and analogues, together with non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, form the primary pharmacologic categories for diabetes management, while antilipemic agents and fibrates address dyslipidemia. Within the insulin family, long‑acting formulations (e.g., insulin glargine, insulin degludec) and intermediate‑ or long‑acting preparations combined with fast‑acting agents (e.g., human insulin aspart, “Insulin, Beef”) constitute the “Insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” subclass; insulin aspart is a rapid‑acting analogue that binds the human insulin‑receptor (INSR) and physiologically decreases lipolysis. GLP‑1 analogues—exenatid, liraglutide and lixisenatide—are non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs; liraglutide and lixisenatide are incorporated into fixed‑dose combinations with long‑acting insulins (insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide), which themselves are classified as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES is a broad therapeutic category that includes the subclass insulins and analogues. Within insulins and analogues, the group Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting is defined as a specific type of long‑acting injectable insulin therapy. This long‑acting group comprises individual members such as insulin glargine and the fixed‑dose combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide. The combination product insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 receptor agonist lixisenatide together with the basal insulin insulin glargine. Together, these hierarchical and compositional relationships clarify how basal insulin agents and basal insulin/GLP‑1 combination products are organized within diabetes pharmacotherapy, informing clinicians’ choices of long‑acting insulin regimens for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1048
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human rapid‑acting insulin analog, exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing lipolysis and achieves this action by targeting the human insulin‑receptor (INSR) protein. Because of its ability to replace or supplement endogenous insulin, insulin aspart is used in the therapeutic management of both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1353
retrieved_docs.content: Insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes that are intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting formulations include insulin aspart, which is classified as a human insulin analog and therefore is a member of this therapeutic group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 309
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin, Beef is classified as a member of the group of insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes that are formulated as intermediate‑ or long‑acting agents combined with fast‑acting insulin, indicating its role as a combined insulin preparation for diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2062
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin glargine and the combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide are both classified as members of the therapeutic group “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting.” This category comprises long‑acting injectable insulin analogues that are used to provide basal insulin coverage in the management of diabetes, helping to maintain stable blood glucose levels over extended periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES comprise two major categories: insulins and analogues and blood glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Both categories are subclasses of the overall drug group used to treat diabetes. Within the insulin and analogue family, several sub‑classes are defined. Insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes that are intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting, as well as insulins and analogues for injection that are long‑acting, are each specific types of insulins and analogues. Long‑acting injectable insulins include insulin glargine, the fixed‑ratio combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, and the fixed‑ratio combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide; each of these products is a member of the long‑acting injectable insulin class. The combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide contains the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutide, and the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide, reflecting the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 308
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, human, is a rapid‑acting insulin analog whose primary physiologic effect is the reduction of Decreased Lipolysis. This effect is mediated through its binding to the INSR protein, human, which serves as the molecular target of insulin aspart. By interacting with the human insulin receptor, insulin aspart suppresses the breakdown of triglycerides in adipose tissue, contributing to improved glucose homeostasis and overall metabolic regulation. This connection highlights the central role of the insulin receptor in mediating the lipolysis‑inhibiting action of insulin aspart in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin glargine / lixisenatide is a fixed‑dose combination medication that contains the active component lixisenatide, a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) receptor agonist, together with insulin glargine; this relationship clarifies that lixisenatide is a constituent of the insulin glargine / lixisenatide formulation, which is important for understanding its pharmacologic composition and therapeutic role in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 267
retrieved_docs.content: non‑insulin class and include liraglutide and lixisenatide, which mimic the action of the endogenous incretin GLP‑1 to enhance insulin secretion and suppress glucagon release. Within the insulin and analogue group, there are subcategories based on duration of action. “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” and “Insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” are both subclasses of insulins and analogues. Specific products belonging to these subclasses include insulin degludec/liraglutide, insulin glargine/lixisenatide, and a formulation of beef insulin; each of these is a member of the respective long‑acting or combined‑with‑fast‑acting insulin class. The combination products illustrate a “part‑of” relationship: insulin degludec/liraglutide contains the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutide, and insulin glargine/lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide. This integration of an insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES encompasses the category insulins and analogues, indicating that insulin preparations and their synthetic analogues are a principal class of medications for diabetes management. Within the broader group of insulins and analogues, there exists a more specific subclass called Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate- or long-acting combined with fast-acting, which refers to injectable insulin formulations that provide basal (intermediate‑ or long‑acting) coverage together with rapid‑acting components for tighter glycemic control. Insulin, Beef is identified as a member of this subclass, meaning it is an injectable insulin product that combines intermediate‑ or long‑acting activity with fast‑acting action. Additionally, Insulin, Beef is characterized as a proteiny, confirming that it is a protein‑based therapeutic agent. Together, these relationships clarify the hierarchical organization of diabetes drugs, the specific role of combined‑action injectable insulins, and the protein nature of the particular product Insulin, Beef.
